content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 21, 2012

Alisha M. Winterswyk
Best Best & Krieger, LLP
18101 Von Kaman Ave., Suite 1000
Irvine, CA 92612

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our file No. A-12-116

Dear Ms. Winterswyk:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Ana Council Member Michele Martinez regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest. Also, the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 72.)

QUESTIONS

1. May Council Member Martinez participate in decisions regarding the Community Maintenance District ("CMD") such as whether to levy annual assessments, disestablish the CMD, or reform the services provided by Downtown Inc. with CMD revenue?

2. Does the public generally exception apply with respect to the CMD assessment decisions?

3. In the event you conclude that Council Member Martinez has a conflict, may the City use the legally required participation exception and re-qualify the Mayor, Council Member Martinez or another Council Member?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulation of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-12-116
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. Council Member Martinez may not make, participate in making, or influence
decisions concerning the CMD because the governmental decisions will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on her real property interest.

2. The public generally exception will only apply if the governmental decision
affects a "significant segment" of the public generally and financially affects the public official's
economic interest in "substantially the same manner" as it affects the significant segment
identified. (Regulation 18707.1 (b)(1) and (b)(2).) You have not provided sufficient facts for us
to make this determination. Accordingly, we are unable to conclude that the exception applies to
Council Member Martinez's situation.

3. It does not appear that the narrowly-construed rule of legally required
participation applies to your facts, given that there is a quorum of council members available to
participate in the decision should they so choose.

FACTS

Michele Martinez is a City Council Member in the City of Santa Ana. She participated in
decisions regarding the formation of a CMD in 2008 and subsequent decisions regarding the
levying of assessments and reformation of the CMD.

In 2011, Council Member Martinez acquired a leasehold interest in a residential unit
located within the boundaries of the CMD. The leasehold interest has a value in excess of
$2,000 and she currently resides in the unit. The CMD assessment has been levied on the
residential unit since 2009. Assessment amounts range from $200 to thousands of dollars
depending on various factors, including the size of the properties, location of the properties and
whether the properties have direct street frontage.

The CMD generates annual revenue of approximately $720,000. The revenue is provided
to a non-profit entity called Downtown Inc., which provides special services to the real
properties within the CMD, including cleaning, security, advertising and special events.
According to a certified assessment engineer, these services render a special benefit to the real
properties within the CMD. The population of Santa Ana is over 3 10,000, and there are more
than 50,000 housing units in the City. Approximately 315 properties are subject to the CMD
assessments.

The Santa Ana City Council consists of six council members and a directly elected
mayor. The Mayor and one Council Member have recused themselves from participating in
CMD decisions, stating that they have conflicts of interest arising from economic interests in
businesses or real property within the boundaries of the CMD, or within 500 feet of the
boundaries of the CMD. Should Council Member Martinez have a disqualifying conflict of
interest and be unable to participate in CMD decisions, only four council members would be
eligible to vote. One of the remaining four council members, however, is facing criminal

File No. A-12-116
Page No. 3

charges related to a personal employment matter and has been absent from City Council
meetings, leaving less than a quorum (only three of seven) of the council members to make
decisions regarding the CMD. By email dated August 14, 2012, you added that although this
Councilperson was present at the last closed session portion of the meeting, it is your
understanding that the individual does not intend to attend the open session portions of the City
Council meetings at this time.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.
Please see the Ehrenkanz Advice Letter, A-10-128, for a full discussion of the eight steps as
applied to a city council member (copy attached).

As a city council member that would be called upon to vote on issues regarding the
CMD, you are a public official that would be making a governmental decision. Thus, you would
meet steps one and two. The remaining steps will be analyzed below.

Step Three: What are Council Member Martinez's economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from certain
enumerated economic interests of a public official. Here, Council Member Martinez has an
economic interest in the real property located within the CMD in which she has a direct interest
of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.2.)

Real Property

Section 82033 describes the circumstances under which a public official has an economic
interest in real property. Here, based on the facts you provided, Council Member Martinez has a
leasehold interest in a residential unit, where she currently resides, valued in excess of $2,000.
An "interest in real property" includes leaseholds. (Section 82033.) Therefore, she has an
economic interest in that real property.

Step 4: Will Council Member Martinez's economic interest be directly or indirectly
involved in decisions she will make, participate in making or influence as a public official?

Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is considered directly
involved in a governmental decision when the decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property in which the official
has an interest. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(4).)

File No. A-12-116
Page No. 4

Your account of the facts indicates that Council Member Martinez's real property interest
is directly involved in the governmental decisions because her property lies within the area
served by the CMD, and the decisions regarding the CMD appear to involve fees assessed on the
properties within the CMD. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(4).)

Step 5: What is the applicable materiality standard?

Any financial effect of a governmental decision on real property in the form of a
leasehold interest that is directly involved in the governmental decision, as here, is presumed to
be material. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(2).) This presumption may be rebutted only by proof that it
is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any effect on any the
following:

"(A) The termination date of the lease;
"(B) The amount of rent paid by the lessee for the leased real property,
either positively or negatively;
"(C) The value of the lessee's right to sublease the real property, either
positively or negatively;
"(D) The legally allowable use or the current use of the real property by
the lessee; or
"(E) The use or enjoyment of the leased real property by the lessee."

You have not provided facts suggesting that the decision would have no financial effect
on the leasehold with respect to the criteria specified above. We therefore conclude that the real
property on which Council Member Martinez's leasehold is situated would be materially
affected.

Step 6: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision on
Council Member Martinez's economic interest will meet the applicable materiality
standard?

Whether a financial effect of a governmental decision is reasonably foreseeable at the
time the decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. The effect of a decision is
"reasonably foreseeable" if it is "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered "reasonably
foreseeable." On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Ibid)

You have indicated that the assessment amounts range from a couple of hundred dollars
to thousands of dollars depending on various factors, and that these revenues provide special
services to the real properties within the CMD, including cleaning, security, advertising and
special events. Indeed, a certified assessment engineer concluded that these services render a
special benefit to the real properties within the CMD. We must therefore conclude that it is
"reasonably foreseeable" that the decisions in question will have a material financial effect on

File No. A-12-116
Page No. 5

Council Member Martinez's leasehold interest within the CMD, such that she will have a
disqualifying conflict of interest.

We next consider whether an exception to the conflict rules will nevertheless permit
Council Member Martinez to vote on the decisions you have described.

Steps 7 and 8: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation.

Public Generally

Even if a public official determines that a decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on his or her economic interest, the official may still participate under
the "public generally" exception if the financial effect of the decision on his or her economic
interests is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation
18707(a).)

Regulation 18707.1(a) establishes a two-pronged test to determine if the material
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally. Under this test, the governmental
decision must affect a "significant segment" of the public generally and must financially affect
the public official's economic interest in "substantially the same manner" as it affects the
significant segment identified. (Regulation 18707.1 (b)(1) and (b)(2).)

For an economic interest in real property, Regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(B) provides that a
governmental decision will affect a "significant segment" of the public generally if the decision
also affects ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the jurisdiction of
the official's agency or the district the official represents, or 5,000 property owners or
homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency.

You have stated that Santa Ana has a population of more than 310,000 and contains more
than 50,000 housing units within the City. Approximately 315 properties are subject to the CMD
assessments. Because Santa Ana Councilmembers are elected at-large her district is the entire
City. Accordingly, the public generally exception does not apply.

Legally Required Participation

Section 87101 permits an official who is otherwise disqualified from making a
governmental decision to participate in the decision when the official's participation is legally
required. For instance, if so many of the council members are disqualified from participating
that there are not enough eligible to make a decision, this exception may be invoked. The rule
does not apply when there is an alternative source of decision-making consistent with the statute
authorizing the decision. (Regulation 18708.) Thus, it only applies when it is legally impossible
for the decision to be made without the participation of the disqualified official, and does not
apply when the disqualified official's vote is merely needed to break a tie or when a quorum can

File No. A-12-116
Page No. 6

be convened of other members of the city council who are not disqualified, whether or not such
other members are actually present at the time of the disqualification.

The Santa Ana City Council is composed of six council members and a directly elected
mayor. Four members constitute a quorum. According to your facts, the Mayor and another
Council Member have already recused themselves from participating in CMD decisions, so if
Council Member Martinez is disqualified, four Council Members will remain eligible to vote.
You also indicated, however, that one of the four remaining Council Members is facing criminal
charges related to a personal employment matter and has been absent from meetings. In your
August 14 email, you added that although this individual was present at the last closed session
portion of the meeting, it is your understanding that the Councilperson does not intend to appear
at the open session portions of the City Council meetings at this time.

Based on these facts alone, we are unable to conclude that the "legally required
participation" exception applies in this instance. There are no facts suggesting this
Councilperson's failure to attend open sessions of meetings is the result of circumstances that
make his or her presence impossible. Indeed, this individual has attended closed session portions
of the meetings. Furthermore, there is no suggestion that the Councilperson's pending criminal
charges disqualify or prevent him or her from participating in decisions concerning the CMD.
As specified in Regulation 18708(c)(2), the narrowly-construed "legally required participation"
exception should not be understood to allow otherwise disqualified public officials to vote, if a
quorum can be convened of other members of the agency who are not disqualified, whether or
not such officials are present at the time of disqualification.

Nevertheless, if you determine at a future point that it is legally impossible to achieve a
quorum, be advised that this exception only allows the participation of the smallest number of
disqualified persons necessary to constitute a quorum. (In re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13.)
The best random method of selecting which disqualified member should participate is by lot.
Other means of random selection that are impartial and equitable may also be used. Whatever
method is used, all disqualified officials must participate in the random selection and all must
have an equal likelihood of being chosen. (Heisinger Advice Letter, No. A-95-333.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Counsel, Legal Division

JW:jgl

Enclosure

participation,. exception applies in this instance. There are no facts suggesting this
